burial.plot_notes: Green Cemetery, Glastonbury, Hartford County, Connecticut.

will.summary: Will of Lt. Benjamin Talcott, Glastonbury CT. Wife: £80 + use of parlor, cellar, garret, ovens, gardens, one-third of orchard and land west of highway to great river — during widowhood only. Son Benjamin: lands in Bolton + 124 acres Hebron (bought of Joseph and John Phelps). Son John: 300 acres Hebron (west of Jacob Root), 50 acres Hebron (north of Richard Curtis), one yoke of steers, £10 moveable, + £15 from Elizur. Son Samuel: south half of home lot, 153 acres beyond Black Ledge River (Glastonbury/Hebron border), half undivided Glastonbury lands, smith-shop with all smithery tools, longest gun, half team. Son Elizur: north half of home lot with all buildings (except smith-shop), 60 acres Ash Swamp Glastonbury, 96 acres near bare hill Glastonbury, other half undivided lands, newest gun, half team — he to pay John £15. Daughter Sarah: £100. Daughter Hannah: £100 + decent suit of clothes or £10. Daughter Mehitabel: £100 + decent suit of clothes or £10. Executors: son-in-law Jonathan Hale and son Benjamin Talcott. Witnesses: Samuel Smith Jr., Nathaniel Talcott, Thomas Wells. Estate inventory: £2563 15s. 8d.
will.note: Will text reproduced in Talcott Pedigree (1876) pp.106-109.

cross_connections.display_label: Glastonbury homestead passed intact to him 175 years later.

narrative_blocks.header: His house bore bullet marks until 1854
narrative_blocks.body: He built a fortified wooden house on Main Street, Glastonbury in 1699 — a refuge from Indian attacks for the surrounding community. The house bore bullet marks until it was taken down soon after 1854.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife Sarah died in childbed at thirty-four
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hollister, granddaughter of original 1636 Hartford settler John Hollister, married him in 1699 and bore him eight children. She died in childbirth at the homestead October 15, 1715.

narrative_blocks.header: Sons signed Bolton's 1731 founding patent
narrative_blocks.body: Two of his sons Benjamin and Nathaniel were listed as Bolton Proprietors in the 1731 patent, signed by their cousin Governor Joseph Talcott. A third son Elizur later chaired Glastonbury's denunciation of the Boston Port Bill.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Wright cousins married two Talcott siblings
narrative_blocks.body: His son Elizur married Ruth Wright; his daughter Mehitabel married Ruth's first cousin Hezekiah Wright. Two Wright cousins thus married two Talcott siblings, weaving the families together.

narrative_blocks.header: Farm in family from 1643 through 1876
narrative_blocks.body: His grandfather John Talcott had bought the Glastonbury farm from Samuel Sherman and Richard Gildersleeve in 1643. The land was still owned by descendant Jared G. Talcott in 1876.

burial.plot_notes: Green Cemetery, Glastonbury, Hartford County, Connecticut.

Wife: £80 + use of parlor, cellar, garret, ovens, gardens, one-third of orchard and land west of highway to great river — during widowhood only. Son John: 300 acres Hebron (west of Jacob Root), 50 acres Hebron (north of Richard Curtis), one yoke of steers, £10 moveable, + £15 from Elizur. Son Samuel: south half of home lot, 153 acres beyond Black Ledge River (Glastonbury/Hebron border), half undivided Glastonbury lands, smith-shop with all smithery tools, longest gun, half team. Son Elizur: north half of home lot with all buildings (except smith-shop), 60 acres Ash Swamp Glastonbury, 96 acres near bare hill Glastonbury, other half undivided lands, newest gun, half team — he to pay John £15. Daughter Sarah: £100. Daughter Hannah: £100 + decent suit of clothes or £10. Daughter Mehitabel: £100 + decent suit of clothes or £10. Executors: son-in-law Jonathan Hale and son Benjamin Talcott. Witnesses: Samuel Smith Jr., Nathaniel Talcott, Thomas Wells. Estate inventory: £2563 15s. 8d.

cross_connections.display_label: Glastonbury homestead passed intact to him 175 years later.

narrative_blocks.header: His house bore bullet marks until 1854
narrative_blocks.body: He built a fortified wooden house on Main Street, Glastonbury in 1699 — a refuge from Indian attacks for the surrounding community. The house bore bullet marks until it was taken down soon after 1854.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife Sarah died in childbed at thirty-four
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hollister, granddaughter of original 1636 Hartford settler John Hollister, married him in 1699 and bore him eight children. She died in childbirth at the homestead October 15, 1715.

narrative_blocks.header: Sons signed Bolton's 1731 founding patent
narrative_blocks.body: Two of his sons Benjamin and Nathaniel were listed as Bolton Proprietors in the 1731 patent, signed by their cousin Governor Joseph Talcott. A third son Elizur later chaired Glastonbury's denunciation of the Boston Port Bill.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Wright cousins married two Talcott siblings
narrative_blocks.body: His son Elizur married Ruth Wright; his daughter Mehitabel married Ruth's first cousin Hezekiah Wright. Two Wright cousins thus married two Talcott siblings, weaving the families together.

narrative_blocks.header: Farm in family from 1643 through 1876
narrative_blocks.body: His grandfather John Talcott had bought the Glastonbury farm from Samuel Sherman and Richard Gildersleeve in 1643. The land was still owned by descendant Jared G. Talcott in 1876.

burial.plot_notes: Green Cemetery, Glastonbury, Hartford County, Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: She died in childbed; the baby followed
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hollister married Deacon Benjamin Talcott and died in childbed at Glastonbury in 1715, at thirty-eight. The daughter whose birth she did not survive, Abigail, lived only eighteen days more.

parents.father_research_notes: Lieut. Samuel Hale Jr. (1645-1711) of Wethersfield CT, son of Samuel Hale Sr. who came from Wales c.1637 and served in the Pequot War; SV gives 'Samuel, Jr., by his wife Mary Edwards.'
parents.mother_research_notes: Mary Edwards (first wife of Samuel Hale Jr., m. 1679, d. Dec 26, 1682).

bio.title: Capt.
bio.bio_blurb: Glastonbury patriarch married to a Talcott

narrative_blocks.header: His family settled Glastonbury for generations
narrative_blocks.body: A Glastonbury man through and through, he married Sarah Talcott and raised eight children there; one son became a Revolutionary War captain. The Hales were remembered locally as people of large stature and great strength.

birth.notes: WikiTree gives Feb 1, 1720-1 (Old Style/New Style transition); 1721 New Style.

death.cause: Camp fever (typhus epidemicus) contracted during Siege of Boston while serving as Captain of 6th Company, Col. Wolcott's Regiment, Connecticut Militia

burial.plot_notes: Buried near Jamaica Plain where the regiment was encamped during siege operations.

military_service.unit: 6th Company, Col. Wolcott's Regiment, Connecticut Militia (19th Regiment)
military_service.notes: Commissioned Ensign of the 4th Company in the 6th Regiment May 1764; Lieutenant October 1768; Captain of the same May 1772. Captain of the 6th Company, Col. Wolcott's Regiment in service 1776 — Siege of Boston, encamped at Jamaica Plain. Died of camp fever (typhus) Mar 7, 1776 at Roxbury Mass. DAR Ancestor #A049261. Will dated Feb 8, 1773, proved May 9, 1776.

narrative_blocks.header: Camp fever killed him at Siege of Boston
narrative_blocks.body: He died March 7, 1776 at Roxbury MA of camp fever — epidemic typhus — while serving as Captain of the 6th Company, Col Wolcott's CT Militia Regiment encamped at Jamaica Plain during the Siege of Boston. He was fifty-five and never saw the Declaration his fellow officers would sign four months later.

narrative_blocks.header: Will mentioned enslaved man named Newport
narrative_blocks.body: His will dated February 8, 1773 — three years before his Revolutionary death — named 'my negro man named Newport' alongside his wife and ten surviving children. The Connecticut Militia captain who died for the Revolution himself held a person enslaved, the contradiction documented in the same will that probated his patriot service.

narrative_blocks.header: Three children died in his epidemic year 1776
narrative_blocks.body: Within months of his March 7 death, his eldest son Jonathan died unmarried October 1, 1776 at age thirty, and his fifteen-year-old daughter Jerusha died September 26, 1776. The Hale household at Glastonbury lost father, son, and daughter in the same Revolutionary year.

narrative_blocks.header: First Talcott in Bolton
narrative_blocks.body: Benjamin Talcott was the first of the Glastonbury Talcotts to move to Bolton, farming land his father Deacon Benjamin had willed him in 1727. As a Bolton Proprietor (1731 patent), he received one-and-a-half parts — his holdings near Bolton Road and the quarry became the base from which the entire Bolton/Vernon line descends. He had twelve children by two wives — Esther Lyman and Deborah Gillett — "from whom most of the Bolton/Vernon line of Talcotts descends."

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve children seeded the Bolton Talcotts
narrative_blocks.body: Granted land in the 1731 Bolton patent, he farmed near the quarry and gave ground to his son beside it. Five children by his first wife and seven by his second became the root of nearly the whole Bolton and Vernon line of the family.

bio.bio_blurb: First Talcott to settle in Bolton

burial.plot_notes: Palisado Cemetery, Windsor, Hartford County CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.
North Bolton Cemetery, Bamforth Road, Bolton CT. Consistent with his land near the quarry on Bolton Road. SOURCE: Tankerhoosen Valley History.

parents.father_research_notes: T00455 Benjamin Talcott (1702-1785) — farmer, Bolton CT.
parents.mother_research_notes: U00046 Esther Lyman (daughter of John Lyman and Mindwell Pomeroy of Northampton MA).

narrative_blocks.header: Farmer in Bolton
narrative_blocks.body: Deacon Benjamin Talcott was a farmer in Bolton, Connecticut, settled on land given to him by his father. He and his wife Elizabeth Lyman had eleven children. Elizabeth died September 3, 1810; Deacon Benjamin died April 18, 1811 — barely seven months after her.

compiler_connection.notes: T00458 Samuel Talcott is the compiler's gen 3 Talcott ancestor. Chain: T00458 → T01001 Samuel → T01389 Samuel × Abigail Pantry Hooker → H01789 Seth → H03203 John Butler → Florence → Helen → Lea → compiler.

primary_residence.notes: Lived and died in Glastonbury on the family homestead.

compiler_connection.notes: T01001 Samuel is the compiler's gen 4 Talcott ancestor. His son T01389 Samuel married Abigail Pantry Hooker — the key Hooker-Talcott intersection.
compiler_connection.path: T01001 → T01389 Samuel × Abigail Pantry Hooker → H01789 Seth → H03203 John Butler → Florence → Helen → Lea → compiler

notable.notable_blurb: Great-grandfather of Adm. George Dewey of Manila Bay

cross_connections.display_label: Both buried in rubble of the 1766 schoolhouse explosion

military_service.notes: FindAGrave Old Eastbury memorial designates him 'Col Samuel Talcott Veteran.' Active in the Revolutionary War period; died Glastonbury CT 16 March 1780, mid-war, leaving his fifteen-year-old son Samuel (T01389) without a father.

burial.plot_notes: Old Eastbury Cemetery, Glastonbury CT. FindAGrave 61929162. Stone surviving intact.

narrative_blocks.header: Revolutionary War colonel from Glastonbury
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel served as a Connecticut militia Colonel in the Revolutionary War — the rank by which his Old Eastbury Cemetery memorial designates him 'Col Samuel Talcott Veteran.' He was the third generation of Talcott military service, his father Capt. Samuel having served in the Connecticut militia of the colonial era and his ancestor Capt. Samuel Talcott having commanded Connecticut dragoons at Deerfield in 1690.

narrative_blocks.header: Four children by Mary, none by Sarah
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel married Mary Smith of Hartford in 1757; she bore him Mary, Hannah, Samuel, and Capt. Nehemiah before her 1770 death at thirty-seven. He remarried Sarah (surname not preserved); she had no children with him and outlived him.

narrative_blocks.header: Died mid-Revolution leaving two young sons
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel died in Glastonbury on 16 March 1780 at age 47, squarely in the middle of the Revolutionary War he had served. Sons Samuel and Nehemiah were just fifteen and thirteen at his death; older daughters Mary and Hannah were already grown. His Glastonbury gravestone survives intact at Old Eastbury Cemetery: 'In Memory of Mr. Samuel Talcott who departed this life March 16th, 1780 Aged 47 years.'

narrative_blocks.header: Descended from the Deerfield dragoon commander
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel's ancestor Capt. Samuel Talcott (b.1635) commanded the Connecticut dragoons at Deerfield Massachusetts at the outbreak of King William's War in 1690. That ancestor's father had served as deputy governor of Connecticut Colony in 1637 and as the colony's treasurer through the 1650s. Three generations of Talcott civic and military service stand between Col. Samuel and the colony's founding.

narrative_blocks.header: Great-grandfather of the Manila Bay admiral
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel's great-grandson Admiral George Dewey commanded the U.S. fleet at the Battle of Manila Bay in May 1898, sinking all eight Spanish ships with no American deaths and only eight wounded. The Talcott military line continued forward from Samuel's Revolutionary service through three generations to one of the most decisive naval victories in American history.

narrative_blocks.header: Survived the 1766 Hartford schoolhouse explosion
narrative_blocks.body: On 23 May 1766 he was among twenty-eight men buried in the rubble when the Hartford schoolhouse exploded — gunpowder ignited during the town's Stamp Act repeal celebration. He survived with severe burns to his face and arms; six others, including Levi Jones, did not.

narrative_blocks.header: Hannah died young
narrative_blocks.body: Hannah died in 1750, aged about fourteen.

narrative_blocks.header: She married the captain of the Hamlin
narrative_blocks.body: Widowed when Christopher Hamlin died in 1768, Abigail married Captain Timothy Starr two years later. A decade on, her second husband was commissioned captain of a privateer sloop that bore the name Hamlin.

cross_connections.display_label: Hooker descendant served in his Troop of horses, 1757 alarm.

narrative_blocks.header: Chaired Glastonbury's denunciation of the Boston Port Bill
narrative_blocks.body: As chairman of the Glastonbury town meeting he led the formal denunciation of the Boston Port Bill — one of the acts that precipitated the Revolution. He already held a commission as Colonel of a Troop of Horse and would lead that troop into the field.

narrative_blocks.header: Carried home from Long Island on a litter
narrative_blocks.body: Present at the Battle of Long Island in August 1776 at age 67, he fell so ill during the campaign that he was carried back to Glastonbury on a litter. He resigned his commission three months later and never rejoined the Continental Army.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost a Susquehanna land claim in court
narrative_blocks.body: He held substantial Connecticut Susquehanna lands in Pennsylvania — claimed under Connecticut's sea-to-sea royal charter. He lost that investment entirely when the courts ruled against the Connecticut claim.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife Ruth brought a large fortune to him
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Ruth Wright brought a substantial fortune to the marriage, inherited from her wealthy father Daniel Wright Jr. They married on his 21st birthday — December 31, 1730 — and son George later served with him in the Revolution.

narrative_blocks.header: He lost four sons to black canker
narrative_blocks.body: Of Colonel Elizur Talcott's twelve children with Ruth Wright, four young sons died of what the family record called 'black canker' between 1748 and 1750. He twice gave a dead son's name to a later child, and only a handful of the twelve lived to marry.

military_service.unit: Sixth Connecticut Militia (Sixth Regiment, Colony of Connecticut)
military_service.notes: Colonel of the Sixth Connecticut Militia 1775-1776. At age 67, present at the Battle of Long Island (Aug 27, 1776). Became ill during the campaign and was carried back to Glastonbury CT on a litter; resigned his commission three months later. Surviving documentation: a holograph order signed by Col. Elizur Talcott in Glastonbury CT June 12, 1776 to Capt. Benjamin Wright of the Fourth Company directing election of a new Lieutenant — the document was signed off by Titus Hosmer for the Lower House (Continental Congress delegate and Articles of Confederation signer) and George Wyllys for the Upper House (Connecticut Secretary of State). See DOC013.

Commanded a Troop of horses raised at Glastonbury for service in the alarm for the relief of Fort William Henry and parts adjacent, August 1757. His unit included Hooker descendant Samuel Hooker Jr (H00228) for 14 days of service. Source: Berlin-Peck Memorial Library — Berlin in the French and Indian War 1753-1763.

burial.plot_notes: Quarryville Cemetery, Bolton CT. FindAGrave Memorial ID 10706544.

narrative_blocks.header: She brought a fortune to her marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Ruth Wright was an only child who inherited a large fortune from her wealthy father and brought it to her marriage with Elizur Talcott. Family memory described her as a remarkably handsome woman, tall and erect, with golden hair and black eyes.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a Bradford of the Mayflower
narrative_blocks.body: George Talcott's first wife, Vienna Bradford, was a direct descendant of Governor William Bradford of the Mayflower. She died in 1785 at age 27. George remarried the following year, had six more children, and lived on the family homestead in Glastonbury — land his family had farmed since his grandfather's 1699 deed.

narrative_blocks.header: At the Long Island retreat with his father
narrative_blocks.body: He served in the Revolution alongside his father Col. Elizur and his brothers, present at the army’s night escape from Long Island. A Glastonbury farmer, he held the homestead that came down through his father.

narrative_blocks.header: His first wife traced to the Mayflower
narrative_blocks.body: He married Vienna Bradford in 1777 — of the Plymouth Bradford line — and lost her at twenty-seven. His second marriage, to Abigail Goodrich, lasted until his death from “inflammation of the brain” in 1813.

bio.bio_blurb: Talcott pioneer settler at Owego, New York

narrative_blocks.header: Her husband's cousin married her brother
narrative_blocks.body: Mehitabel Talcott married Hezekiah Wright on November 29, 1733. Three years earlier, her brother Elizur Talcott had married Hezekiah's first cousin Ruth Wright. Two Wright cousins married two Talcott siblings — children of Deacon Benjamin Talcott and grandchildren of John Hollister's line on both sides.

political_offices.title: Deputy to General Court
political_offices.notes: Secretary of the General Court in absence of Col. Allyn during October session 1684

political_offices.title: Member, Standing Committee
political_offices.notes: Appointed to order measures in intervals of General Court

political_offices.title: Captain of the Troop, Hartford County
Assistant
political_offices.notes: From 1683 except during Andros' administration, was Assistant until his death

will.summary: Will of Captain Samuel Talcott, Wethersfield CT. Bequeathed to sons: Samuel (home lot south, great meadow parcels, Westfield lot); John (home lot north, 5 acres great meadow, 12 acres Westfield); Elizur (Westfield brook land, 6 acres meadow, 50-acre lot near Farmington line); Joseph (30 acres Westfield); Benjamin (north half, east side Connecticut River, undivided Indian lands, south half 200-acre court grant); Nathaniel (south half Nawburk lot, undivided east-river land, north half 200-acre grant, one cow). Daughters: Hannah Chester £90; Rachel £80. Wife Mary: £10/yr, use of lower room, garden, one cow during widowhood. Executor: son Samuel Talcott. Overseers: Capt. Robert Wells, Ensign Samuel Butler.
will.note: Original will text reproduced in Talcott Pedigree (1876) pp.97-102.

T00036.notable_legacy: "From these two sons, John and Samuel, are descended all of the name of TALCOTT in America." — Talcott Pedigree (1876). All living Talcotts in America descend from Capt. Samuel and/or his brother John (T00035).

cross_connections.display_label: founded Hartford; lines joined via Hannah Holyoke
Capt. Samuel Talcott's wife Hannah Holyoke was the daughter of.

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard College 1658
narrative_blocks.body: Harvard College 1658 — the scholar of the Talcott family; his father bequeathed all his books to Samuel (except the 'Martyr Book' given to John)

narrative_blocks.header: One of the original proprietors of the town
narrative_blocks.body: One of the original proprietors of the town of Glastonbury, CT — owned the lot purchased by his father John in 1643 of Samuel Sherman and Richard Gildersleeve, 44 rods wide running three miles to the wilderness; still owned by descendants at time of writing

narrative_blocks.header: Captain of the Troop, Hartford County 1681
narrative_blocks.body: Captain of the Troop, Hartford County 1681; Assistant of the Colony 1683-1691

narrative_blocks.header: From John and Samuel Talcott are descended ALL
narrative_blocks.body: From John and Samuel Talcott are descended ALL of the name Talcott in America

narrative_blocks.header: Direct ancestor of the compiler Samuel Talcott Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: Direct ancestor of the compiler Samuel Talcott Hooker through the maternal Talcott line

military_service.notes: Rose through Trained Band ranks during this period

burial.plot_notes: Buried in Hartford. Name inscribed on monument erected by citizens of Hartford to perpetuate memory of the founders of the Colony of Connecticut.

political_offices.title: Select Man (Selectman)
political_offices.notes: Chosen at general town meeting Feb 4, 1634 along with Haynes, Bradstreet, and four others

political_offices.title: Representative
political_offices.notes: One of Representatives for Newtown (Cambridge), May 14, 1634

emigration.notes: 123 passengers including 50 children; all arrived in good health after 12-week passage. Fellow passengers included William Wadsworth, Tobie Willet (possible relation to Mary Willet who married Rev. Samuel Hooker), James Olmstedd, and others of Hooker's Company.

hartford_land.notes: Nine parcels originally granted: dwelling house on east side of Main Street abutting Centinel Hill; seven acres east of Great River; five acres in North Meadow; forty-seven acres meadow/swamp in North Meadow; one acre in Little Meadow; thirty acres in Old Oxpasture; seventeen acres in Cow Pasture; eleven acres in Venturers Field; sixteen acres in Neck of Land. Also gained 25 additional parcels by acquisition.

will.bequests_summary: Dwelling house and lands to wife Dorothy for her natural life; then to son John. Wethersfield property to son Samuel. Grandchildren John Russell (£20), John Talcott, Samuel Talcott, Elizabeth Talcott (£5 each). £5 to Rev. Samuel Stone; £5 toward a Latin school at Hartford. Overseers: Richard Lord Sr. and son John.
will.gift_to_stone: Five pounds to 'my reverend and beloved teacher, Mr. Stone' — Rev. Samuel Stone, Hartford minister and colleague of Thomas Hooker

narrative_blocks.body: The only Talcott known to have emigrated to America — all American Talcotts descend from him

narrative_blocks.body: Sailed on ship Lyon June 22, 1632 with Hooker's Company, arriving Boston Sept 16, 1632

narrative_blocks.header: Among the 'Adventurers Party' of 25 men who
narrative_blocks.body: Among the 'Adventurers Party' of 25 men who explored the Hartford area October 1635, prior to Hooker's party departure May 1636

narrative_blocks.header: Fifth greatest proprietor of houses and lands
narrative_blocks.body: Fifth greatest proprietor of houses and lands in Newtown (Cambridge) 1634

narrative_blocks.header: His carpenter Nicholas Clark built the first house
narrative_blocks.body: His carpenter Nicholas Clark built the first house in Hartford on the ground where the North Church (Dr. Bushnell's) later stood

narrative_blocks.header: Was styled 'The Worshipful Mr. John Talcott'
narrative_blocks.body: Was styled 'The Worshipful Mr. John Talcott' in colonial records

narrative_blocks.header: Mother's daughter Rachel married Hartford Founder John S…
narrative_blocks.body: His mother's daughter Rachel married Hartford Founder John Steele; her sister Sarah married Hartford Founder William Wadsworth; he was an uncle of Hartford Founder John Skinner

bio.notes: The only Talcott known to have emigrated to America. Co-founder of Hartford with Rev. Thomas Hooker.

parents.discrepancy_note: One source gives parents as 'John Mott and Alice Harington'; Talcott 1876 gives 'Mark Mott and Frances Gutter' with Mark being son of Thomas and Alice (Mead) Mott. The Talcott 1876 reconstruction preferred as more detailed.
parents.mother_research_notes: Probably Frances Gutter (wife of Mark Mott). Frances died Braintree, buried Feb 23, 1615. Mark Mott died Braintree, buried Dec 14, 1637.
parents.father_research_notes: Probably Mark Mott, son of Thomas and Alice (Mead) Mott of Braintree, Essex. Talcott 1876 says 'supposed to have been the daughter of Mark, son of Thomas and Alice (Mead) Mott.'

parents.mother_research_notes: Prudence, daughter of Rev. John Stockton of Kinholdt, England; married Edward Holyoke June 18, 1612

narrative_blocks.header: He married Springfield’s founders, then linked Hartford’s
narrative_blocks.body: Crossing from Tamworth, England, Elizur Holyoke married Mary Pynchon, daughter of Springfield's founder William Pynchon, and rose to prominent office in the Massachusetts colony. His daughter Hannah's marriage to Captain Samuel Talcott then joined the founding families of Springfield and Hartford.

parents.notes: Daughter of Hon. William Pynchon of Springfield, MA

burial.plot_notes: Springfield MA — gravestone still standing 1876 per Talcott Pedigree.
